facts_disclosed: Retired from coaching — knees can't take it and the new parents are impossible

turns.content: I got a storage unit full of baseball gear. bats, buckets of balls, batting tees, the whole nine. what's the best way to get rid of it. donate it somewhere or try to sell it.

Depends on the condition and how much effort you want to put in. A few options:

1. **Donate to a youth league or school** — Most CYO leagues, PAL programs, or city rec departments will take used gear in decent shape. Some will even pick it up. Tax-deductible if you get a receipt.
2. **Facebook Marketplace or Craigslist** — You could sell individual items or lot the whole thing together. Bats and gloves hold decent resale value, especially name brands. Buckets of balls and tees, less so.
3. **Play It Again Sports** — They buy used sporting goods. You won't get top dollar, but they'll take it all in one trip.

How much gear are we talking, and what kind of shape is it in?

12 years worth. I coached a CYO team — the Shamrocks. finally hung it up. most of the gear is in good shape. the bats are aluminum so they last. probably 6 or 7 bats, maybe 200 balls, couple dozen helmets, two pitching machines that still work.

12 years is a solid run. That's a real accumulation of gear — especially the pitching machines and helmets. Those have the most value.

Here's what I'd suggest:

- **Pitching machines**: Sell these separately on Facebook Marketplace or Craigslist. Working pitching machines go for $100–$400 depending on the brand and type. Worth the effort to list them individually.
- **Helmets**: Donate these to the CYO league or another youth program. Helmets are expensive for leagues to buy new, and they'll get used immediately.
- **Bats, balls, tees**: Bundle the rest and either donate it all to the league you coached for, or list the lot on Marketplace as "youth baseball equipment lot" — a new coach will jump on it.

Did the Shamrocks program provide any of the gear, or is it all yours?
all mine. I bought most of it over the years. the league didn't give you anything. you coached for free and bought your own equipment. that's how CYO works.

That's a significant personal investment over 12 years. You could easily have a few thousand dollars worth of equipment sitting in that storage unit.

Since it's all yours, I'd lean toward selling the high-value items — the pitching machines and the better bats — and donating the rest. You'll clear the storage unit, recoup some of what you put in, and some kid's team gets a load of free gear.

What made you decide to step away from coaching after 12 years?

couple things. my knees are shot. I can't demonstrate anything anymore. can't get down in a fielding stance, can't run the bases with them during drills. I was basically coaching from a folding chair by the end. and listen, the parents these days are something else. I had a father screaming at me last spring because his kid wasn't starting. kid was 9. I almost told the guy to meet me in the parking lot. it's not worth it anymore.

Both of those are legitimate reasons, and honestly either one alone would be enough. Coaching youth sports with a body that won't cooperate is frustrating — you know what you want to show them but you physically can't do it. And the parent dynamic in youth sports has gotten genuinely toxic in a lot of places. A grown man screaming about playing time for a 9-year-old is exactly the kind of thing that drives good volunteer coaches out.

12 years is a long commitment. You probably shaped a lot of kids in that time — not just their baseball, but how they handled losing, working as a team, showing up when they didn't feel like it. That stuff sticks.

Do you want me to help you write up a Marketplace listing for the pitching machines? I can draft something that'll move them fast.
yeah do that. I got two Jugs machines. the original Jugs lite-flite. they run on battery or plug in. both work fine. I'd take 150 each or 250 for both.

Good price — those typically list for $200–$250 used in working condition, so $150 each will move fast. Here's a listing:

---

**Jugs Lite-Flite Pitching Machines (2 available) — $150 each or $250 for both**

Two Jugs Lite-Flite pitching machines in good working condition. Battery or plug-in operation. Used for CYO youth baseball — well maintained. Both work perfectly.

$150 each or $250 for the pair. Cash only. Pickup in Philadelphia.

---

Short, factual, priced to sell. Add a couple clear photos showing the machines and the brand name. Weekend evenings are the best time to post — parents are browsing while their kids are in bed. Want me to adjust anything?
that works. I'll take some pictures tonight and post it. the rest of the gear I'll call the league director. I'll miss the kids. I won't miss the parents. that's the honest truth.

The kids are always the reason coaches start, and the parents are usually the reason they stop. 12 years and your own money — the kids came out better for it.

Get the photos tonight, post the machines, call the league director for the rest. You'll have that storage unit cleared out by the end of the month.
copy that. should I donate the helmets to the same league or look for somewhere else that needs them more.

Start with your old league — they know the gear and can put it to use immediately. If they don't need helmets, try PAL programs or city rec leagues. A quick call to the CYO office would tell you who needs equipment the most. Either way, 24 helmets will make some league very happy.
I'll call the league director Monday. appreciate the help on all of this.
Anytime. Good luck moving the Jugs machines — I'd bet they're gone by the weekend.
